title: Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0 — Part 1: Teacher AI Competency Framework
sourceDocument: AI Competency Framework — For Eduversal Partner Schools (v1.0 PDF)
sourcePart: Part One: Teacher AI Competency Framework

_meta.purpose: Verbatim canonical content for Part 1 of the Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0. This file is the SOURCE OF TRUTH — practical-layer playbooks reference these blocks via referenceAnchor / refId pattern teacher.{level}.{domain}. Do not edit verbatim text inline in derivative content (Common Mistake #9).
_meta.chipFamilyResolution: Chip refIds use pattern teacher.{level}.{domain}, e.g. teacher.foundation.domainA. cambridge-crossref.js (Phase 1b) reads this file to populate AICF chip popovers.
_meta.writingNote: Verbatim from PDF. Lightly structured into JSON; text content unchanged. Where the PDF used run-on prose, paragraphs are preserved as array entries for cleaner display.

overview.summary: The Teacher AI Competency Framework defines three progressive levels of AI competency: Foundation, Practitioner, and Leader. Each level builds upon the previous, representing increasing sophistication in AI knowledge, application skills, and professional contribution.
overview.expectation: All teaching staff are expected to achieve the Foundation level. Progression to Practitioner and Leader levels reflects growing expertise and expanded professional responsibility for AI integration.

competencyDomains._overview: Teacher AI competency is assessed across five domains.

domains.title: AI Knowledge and Understanding
domains.scope: Theoretical knowledge about AI, including how it works, its capabilities and limitations, and its implications for education and society.

domains.title: Practical Application
domains.scope: The ability to effectively use AI tools for professional tasks, including lesson planning, resource creation, assessment, and administration.

domains.title: Pedagogical Integration
domains.scope: Skill in incorporating AI meaningfully into teaching practice to enhance student learning outcomes.

domains.title: Critical Evaluation
domains.scope: The capacity to assess AI outputs, identify errors and biases, and make informed judgments about appropriate use.

domains.title: Professional Contribution
domains.scope: Sharing expertise, supporting colleagues, and contributing to school and network AI development.

foundation.summary: The foundation level represents the essential AI competencies required of all teaching staff. Teachers at this level understand AI basics, can use approved tools for core professional tasks, and operate within policy guidelines.
foundation.expectedTimeline: All teaching staff should achieve Foundation level within the first year of framework implementation.

domainA.title: AI Knowledge and Understanding — Foundation
verbatim: Teachers at the Foundation level demonstrate understanding of basic AI concepts, including what artificial intelligence is and how it differs from traditional software. They can explain in simple terms how generative AI tools like ChatGPT produce outputs. They understand that AI systems learn from training data and may reflect biases present in that data.

Teachers recognise AI capabilities relevant to education, including text generation, summarisation, translation, and question answering. They understand key limitations of current AI, including the tendency to produce confident but incorrect information, lack of true understanding or reasoning, inability to access real-time information (unless specifically designed to do so), and potential for biased or inappropriate outputs.

Teachers are aware of ethical considerations around AI use in education, including academic integrity, data privacy, and equity of access. They understand the school's AI policies and their responsibilities under those policies.

domainB.title: Practical Application — Foundation
verbatim: Teachers at Foundation level can access and use approved AI tools for basic professional tasks. They can write clear, effective prompts to generate useful outputs for lesson planning, including learning objectives, activity ideas, and discussion questions.

Teachers can use AI to create basic instructional materials such as explanations, examples, practice problems, and vocabulary lists. They can use AI to support administrative tasks including drafting communications, summarising notes, and organising information.

Teachers consistently review AI outputs before use, checking for factual accuracy, curriculum alignment, and appropriateness. They can identify obvious errors or inappropriate content in AI-generated materials. They refine and customise AI outputs rather than using them verbatim.
Teachers follow data privacy requirements, never entering identifiable student information into public AI platforms. They use only approved AI tools for professional work.

domainC.title: Pedagogical Integration — Foundation
verbatim: Teachers at Foundation level use AI-generated materials to support planned learning objectives. They adapt AI outputs to suit their specific student population, context, and curriculum requirements.

Teachers maintain their professional role as instructional decision-makers, using AI as a tool rather than a replacement for pedagogical judgment. They ensure AI-enhanced lessons still require active student thinking and engagement.
Teachers can explain to students, in age-appropriate terms, when AI has been used in creating materials or activities.

domainD.title: Critical Evaluation — Foundation
verbatim: Teachers at Foundation level consistently fact-check AI-generated content against authoritative sources. They can identify common AI errors including factual inaccuracies, anachronisms, false citations, and logical inconsistencies.

Teachers recognise when AI output is generic or superficial and requires substantial enhancement. They can identify content that may be culturally inappropriate or biased and make necessary adjustments.
Teachers understand that AI outputs require professional judgment to evaluate and should not be accepted uncritically.

domainE.title: Professional Contribution — Foundation
verbatim: Teachers at Foundation level engage positively with school AI initiatives and professional development opportunities. They share basic experiences and tips with immediate colleagues informally.
Teachers ask questions and seek support when uncertain about appropriate AI use. They provide feedback on AI tools and policies when requested.

practitioner.summary: Practitioner level represents proficient AI integration, where teachers use AI strategically to enhance teaching effectiveness and student outcomes. Teachers at this level demonstrate sophisticated application skills and consistent positive impact on practice.
practitioner.expectedTimeline: The majority of teaching staff should achieve Practitioner level within two years of framework implementation.

domainA.title: AI Knowledge and Understanding — Practitioner
verbatim: Teachers at Practitioner level demonstrate deeper understanding of how AI language models work, including concepts like training data, parameters, and probability-based generation. They understand why AI produces certain types of errors and can anticipate likely limitations.

Teachers stay current with developments in educational AI, understanding new tools and capabilities as they emerge. They can explain AI concepts clearly to students and parents, addressing common misconceptions.

Teachers understand the broader implications of AI for education, including potential impacts on assessment, curriculum, and the skills students need for future success. They are familiar with research and best practices in educational AI integration.

domainB.title: Practical Application — Practitioner
verbatim: Teachers at Practitioner level demonstrate advanced prompt engineering skills, crafting detailed prompts that produce high-quality, targeted outputs. They can use techniques like providing examples, specifying format and tone, and iterating through multiple prompts to refine results.

Teachers use AI efficiently across a wide range of professional tasks, including creating differentiated materials for diverse learners, developing assessment items aligned with specific objectives, generating detailed rubrics and success criteria, producing comprehensive feedback templates, and supporting curriculum planning and unit development.

Teachers can troubleshoot when AI tools do not produce desired results, adjusting approaches to achieve better outcomes. They evaluate new AI tools critically to assess potential value for their practice.

domainC.title: Pedagogical Integration — Practitioner
verbatim: Teachers at Practitioner level integrate AI strategically to address specific pedagogical challenges and enhance learning outcomes. They use AI to enable differentiation that would otherwise be impractical, creating personalised materials for individual students or groups.

Teachers design learning experiences that leverage AI capabilities while maintaining rigorous expectations for student thinking and learning. They teach students to use AI responsibly as part of subject instruction, modelling critical evaluation and ethical use.
Teachers can articulate clear pedagogical rationale for AI integration choices, explaining how AI use serves specific learning objectives.

domainD.title: Critical Evaluation — Practitioner
verbatim: Teachers at Practitioner level apply sophisticated evaluation criteria to AI outputs, assessing not only accuracy but also depth, nuance, and pedagogical suitability. They can identify subtle biases and limitations that might not be immediately obvious.

Teachers make informed judgments about when AI is and is not appropriate for particular tasks. They critically evaluate claims about AI capabilities and educational applications, distinguishing evidence-based practices from hype.
Teachers can evaluate AI-related academic integrity concerns, identifying potential misuse while avoiding false accusations.

domainE.title: Professional Contribution — Practitioner
verbatim: Teachers at Practitioner level actively share effective AI practices with colleagues through both informal collaboration and formal channels. They contribute to department or school-level discussions about AI integration.
Teachers support less experienced colleagues in developing AI skills, providing guidance and encouragement. They participate in professional learning communities focused on AI in education.
Teachers provide constructive feedback on school AI policies and practices, contributing to continuous improvement.

leader.summary: Leader level represents advanced expertise and professional leadership in AI integration. Teachers at this level shape practice beyond their own classroom, contributing to school and network development.
leader.expectedTimeline: Identified teacher leaders should achieve this level within 18 months of framework implementation. Not all teachers are expected to reach Leader level; it represents a specialist professional contribution.

domainA.title: AI Knowledge and Understanding — Leader
verbatim: Teachers at Leader level demonstrate comprehensive understanding of AI technology, educational applications, and implications for the future of schooling. They can engage with technical concepts and research literature at a sophisticated level.

Teachers anticipate future developments in AI and their potential educational implications, helping schools prepare proactively. They understand policy and regulatory landscapes related to AI in education.
Teachers serve as authoritative resources on AI questions within the school community, providing accurate information and thoughtful perspective.

domainB.title: Practical Application — Leader
verbatim: Teachers at Leader level demonstrate expert-level AI application skills, modelling exemplary practice for colleagues. They can design complex AI-enhanced workflows that significantly improve professional efficiency and effectiveness.

Teachers evaluate and recommend AI tools for school adoption, conducting thorough assessment against educational and technical criteria. They develop resources, templates, and guides that help colleagues use AI more effectively.
Teachers troubleshoot complex AI implementation challenges, supporting colleagues who encounter difficulties.

domainC.title: Pedagogical Integration — Leader
verbatim: Teachers at Leader level design innovative AI-enhanced learning experiences that serve as models for colleagues. They develop curriculum materials and units that integrate AI meaningfully and can be shared across the school or network.

Teachers lead development of student AI literacy curriculum, ensuring systematic and age-appropriate instruction. They contribute to assessment practices that maintain rigour and integrity in an AI-enabled environment.
Teachers mentor colleagues in pedagogical AI integration, providing observation, feedback, and coaching.

domainD.title: Critical Evaluation — Leader
verbatim: Teachers at Leader level contribute to school and network evaluation of AI tools, policies, and practices. They analyse AI integration outcomes, identifying what is working well and what needs adjustment.
Teachers lead discussions about ethical dimensions of AI in education, helping colleagues navigate complex issues. They contribute to academic integrity policy development and implementation.
Teachers evaluate emerging AI developments critically, advising school leadership on opportunities and risks.

domainE.title: Professional Contribution — Leader
verbatim: Teachers at Leader level lead professional development sessions on AI topics for colleagues at school and network level. They facilitate professional learning communities focused on AI integration.

Teachers contribute to school and network AI policy development, bringing practitioner perspective to strategic discussions. They represent the school in external forums and networks related to educational AI.

Teachers document and share effective practices through written resources, presentations, or other appropriate formats. They may contribute to broader professional discourse through conference presentations, publications, or online communities.

Teachers should complete self-assessment against this framework annually, typically at the start of the academic year. Self-assessment involves reviewing the competency descriptors for each domain and level, identifying the level that best describes current practice, noting specific areas of strength and areas for growth, and setting goals for the coming year.
Self-assessment should be honest and reflective. The goal is professional growth, not performance judgment.

Self-assessment should be discussed with line managers during professional development conversations. Verification may include review of AI-enhanced materials and resources, classroom observation of AI-integrated lessons, colleague feedback and peer observation, and evidence of professional contribution.
Formal level certification is not required, but schools should track overall staff competency distribution to inform professional development planning.
Based on self-assessment, teachers should identify specific professional learning goals, activities to address identified growth areas, resources and support needed, and timeline for achieving goals.
School leaders should ensure professional development offerings address needs across all competency levels and domains.

_indexing._purpose: Quick lookup table for chip-popover resolution and reader-page indexing.
_indexing.competencyBlockMatrix: 3 levels × 5 domains = 15 core competency blocks + 1 overview + 1 domain overview + 3 level summaries + 1 assessment block = 21 referenceable blocks
